content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 630 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 20, 2017

Kristen DePaul
Modoc County Assessor/Recorder
204 S. Court Street, Room 106
Alturas, CA 96101

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-077

Dear Ms. DePaul:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of
Government Code Section 1090.¹ Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law,
including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact
when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes
your facts are complete and accurate.

As required by Section 1097.1(c)(3), we have forwarded your request to the Attorney
General's Office and the Modoc County District Attorney's Office. We did not receive a written
response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for
purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against
any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTIONS

1. Does Section 1090 require the cancellation of an existing contract between Modoc
County and your husband's veterinary clinic for animal control, boarding, and treatment, given that
you are the recently appointed Modoc County Assessor/Recorder, and that your husband's
veterinary clinic entered into the contract with the County prior to your appointment?

2. Would Section 1090 prohibit your husband's veterinary clinic from making or
participating in the making of, or the County from entering into, a potential modification, extension,
or renegotiation of that contract?

CONCLUSIONS

1. No. Section 1090 does not require the cancellation of that contract because it was made
prior to your appointment.

2. No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the County from entering into a new contract for
animal control, boarding, and treatment with MVC so long you refrain from making or participating
in the making of that contract in your official capacity as Assessor/Recorder.

¹ All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-077
Page No. 2

FACTS

You took office as the Modoc County Assessor/Recorder in January. Your husband and a
partner own and operate all the veterinary clinics located within the County, including the Modoc
Veterinary Center (MVC) in Alturas and satellite clinics in Adin and Cedarville. In October of
2014, prior to your appointment as Assessor/Recorder, MVC entered into a contract with the
County to provide animal control, boarding, and treatment services when an animal is caught within
the County after hours or has no discernable owner (the "animal-control contract").

ANALYSIS

The Animal-Control Contract

Section 1090 provides that public officers or employees "shall not be financially interested
in any contract made by them in their official capacity." A contract that violates Section 1090 is
void, and the prohibition applies regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and
equitable to all parties.² Under Section 1090, an official has an interest in the community and
separate property income of the official's spouse.³

An official who has contracted in his or her private capacity with the government agency
before the official is elected or appointed does not violate the section; the official may continue in
his or her position as the contracting party for the duration of that contract; and the official's
election or appointment does not void it.⁴

MVC entered into the animal-control contract with the County in October 2014. You took
office as the Assessor/Recorder in January. Therefore, MVC may continue providing animal
control, boarding, and treatment services under the animal-control contract for that contract's
duration without violating Section 1090. Accordingly, we conclude that Section 1090 does not
require the animal-control contract's cancellation.

Modification, Extension, or Renegotiation of the Animal-Control Contract

Because a decision to modify, extend, or renegotiate a contract constitutes involvement in
the making of a new contract under Section 1090,⁵ the potential for a violation of the statute would
arise again if the County modifies, extends, or renegotiates the animal-control contract.

As noted above, Section 1090 prohibits an official from being "financially interested in any
contract made by them in their official capacity." Whether an officer or employee has participated

² Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646-649

³ Nielsen v. Richards (1925) 75 Cal.App. 680; Thorpe v. Long Beach Community College Dist. (2000) 83
Cal.App.4th 655; 89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 69 (2006).

⁴ Beaudry v. Valdez (1867) 32 Cal. 269; 85 Ops.Cal Atty.Gen. 176 (2002); 84 Ops.Cal Atty.Gen. 34 (2001).

⁵ See City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191.

File No. A-17-077
Page No. 3

in the making of a contract in his or her official capacity was examined in County of Marin v.
Dufficy (1956) 144 Cal.App.2d 30, where a county physician leased space in his medical building to
the county. The court stated:

"In the instant case, it is clear that Dr. Dufficy and his wife executed the lease
as private citizens who owned an office building in which the county desired to
lease four rooms. They were in no sense acting in an 'official capacity'...."⁶

Following the reasoning of the court's decision in Dufficy, the Attorney General has held in
numerous opinions that an officer or employee contracting solely in his or her private capacity with
either his or her public entity or another public entity did not violate Section 1090. The Attorney
General has determined that: firefighters were permitted to sell a product, which they invented in
their private capacity, to their fire department so long as they did not participate in the sale in their
official capacities;⁷ county officers were permitted to bid on the county's surplus property at a
public auction so long as they did not participate or influence the decisions pertaining to the sale, or
the purchase of the property, in their official capacities;⁸ and a California Youth Authority board
member was permitted to obtain surplus property from the Department of Water Resources because
the member did not participate in either the sale or purchase in his official capacity.⁹

Consistent with the court's decision in Dufficy and the reasoning in the Attorney General's
opinions described above, we conclude that the County may enter into a new contract for animal
control, boarding, and treatment with MVC without violating Section 1090, so long you refrain
from making or participating in the making of that contract in your official capacity as
Assessor/Recorder.¹⁰

⁶ Id. at p. 37.

⁷ 80 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 41 (1997).

⁸ 63 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 19 (1980).

⁹ 53 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 163 (1970).

¹⁰ Because governmental decisions relating to the potential modification, extension, or renegotiation of the
animal control contract would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your source-of-income interest
in MVC, the Political Reform Act would prohibit you from making, participating in making, or attempting to use your
official position to influence those decisions. (Sections 87100 and 87103; Regulations 18701, 18702.3 and 18704.)
Accordingly, the Act requires you to recuse yourself from those decisions as provided in Regulation 18707.

File No. A-17-077
Page No. 4

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

sections.question:
1. Does Section 1090 require the cancellation of an existing contract between Modoc 
County and your husband's veterinary clinic for animal control, boarding, and treatment, given that 
you are the recently appointed Modoc County Assessor/Recorder, and that your husband's 
veterinary clinic entered into the contract with the County prior to your appointment? 
2. Would Section 1090 prohibit your husband's veterinary clinic from making or 
participating in the making of, or the County from entering into, a potential modification, extension, 
or renegotiation of that contract?
sections.conclusion:
1. No. Section 1090 does not require the cancellation of that contract because it was made 
prior to your appointment. 
2. No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the County from entering into a new contract for 
animal control, boarding, and treatment with MVC so long you refrain from making or participating 
in the making of that contract in your official capacity as Assessor/Recorder. 
1
